---
title: "Acesulfame Potassium (INS 950)"
slug: acesulfame-potassium-ins-950
category: "Additives & Functional"
category-slug: additives-functional
keywords: ["Acesulfame Potassium", "Ace-K", "INS 950", "Artificial Sweetener", "Non-caloric", "Sugar Substitute", "Heat-stable"]
description: "Acesulfame Potassium, or Ace-K (INS 950), is a synthetic, non-caloric sweetener discovered in 1967"
---

## History and Sourcing

Acesulfame [Potassium](potassium.qmd), or Ace-K (INS 950), is a synthetic, non-caloric sweetener discovered in 1967. It lacks traditional Indian culinary roots, being a modern introduction driven by global health and diet trends. Unlike natural sweeteners, it is synthetically produced and not sourced from agricultural regions, reflecting its chemical origin rather than botanical heritage.

## Home Kitchen Use

In Indian home kitchens, Acesulfame [Potassium](potassium.qmd) is not a traditional ingredient. It is primarily used indirectly by consumers through commercially available 'sugar-free' or 'diet' products. Home cooks might incorporate sugar substitutes containing Ace-K into recipes for diet-friendly beverages, desserts, or baked goods, leveraging its heat stability for various cooking applications.

## Industrial Applications

Industrially, Ace-K is a widespread ingredient in the Indian FMCG sector. It serves as a non-nutritive sweetener in diet soft drinks, sugar-free chewing gums, candies, dairy products, baked goods, and pharmaceuticals. Its heat stability and synergistic effect with other high-intensity sweeteners like sucralose or aspartame are key functional properties, enhancing sweetness and masking off-notes in processed foods.

## Distinctions and Common Confusion

Consumers often confuse Acesulfame [Potassium](potassium.qmd) with other sweeteners. It is a synthetic compound, distinct from natural sweeteners like stevia or monk fruit. While an artificial sweetener like aspartame or sucralose, Ace-K is more heat-stable than aspartame, making it suitable for cooking and baking. It also generally avoids the metallic aftertaste sometimes associated with saccharin, especially when used in synergistic blends.
